one_liner: A 52-year-old female with a history of poorly controlled hypertension presents with severe headache, blurred vision, and a blood pressure of 220/130 mmHg.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient presents to the emergency department with a severe occipital headache that began 4 hours ago, described as the worst headache of her life. She also notes blurred vision, nausea, and mild chest discomfort. She ran out of her blood pressure medications 2 weeks ago and has not been taking them. She denies focal weakness, speech difficulty, or seizures.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension (20 years, poorly controlled), chronic kidney disease stage 3, type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity
stage2.medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily, losartan 100 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg BID (all non-compliant for 2 weeks)
stage2.family_history: Mother had hemorrhagic stroke at age 65. Father had CKD and dialysis. Both parents hypertensive.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. No alcohol. Works as a home health aide. High-sodium diet. Lives alone with 2 teenage children.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Anxious-appearing female in moderate distress, holding head. HEENT: Fundoscopic exam reveals bilateral papilledema, flame-shaped hemorrhages, and cotton-wool spots (Grade III-IV hypertensive retinopathy). No JVD. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular, S4 gallop, loud A2, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Bibasilar fine crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no bruits. Extremities: Trace bilateral edema. Neurological: Alert and oriented, no focal deficits, deep tendon reflexes 3+ throughout.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Acute worsening from baseline, indicating hypertensive nephrosclerosis and acute kidney injury
Mildly elevated, suggesting demand ischemia from severe hypertension
Elevated, indicating myocardial strain from acute pressure overload

lab_results.result: 2+ protein, no RBC casts, no WBCs

lab_results.clinical_significance: Microangiopathic changes from severe hypertension damaging small vessels
Mild anemia, possibly from CKD or microangiopathic hemolysis
Normal, though low-normal may suggest mild consumptive process

imaging_results.result: Sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm, LVH with strain pattern (ST depression and T-wave inversions in lateral leads), no acute ST elevation
imaging_results.clinical_significance: LVH with strain pattern from chronic hypertension; no STEMI

imaging_results.result: Cardiomegaly, mild pulmonary vascular congestion, no pleural effusions
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Cardiomegaly and early pulmonary edema from acute pressure overload

imaging_results.result: No intracranial hemorrhage, no acute infarct, no mass lesion
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Rules out hemorrhagic stroke as cause of headache

stage3.key_tests_rationale: BMP assesses for hypertensive nephrosclerosis and AKI. Troponin evaluates for myocardial injury. Urinalysis detects proteinuria/hematuria. ECG screens for ischemia. CT head rules out stroke in a patient with headache and severely elevated BP.

stage5.final_clue: Severely elevated BP at 220/130 mmHg with evidence of acute end-organ damage: hypertensive retinopathy (papilledema, hemorrhages), acute kidney injury (creatinine rise from baseline), troponin elevation, and pulmonary edema. This defines a hypertensive emergency.

debrief.classic_presentation: Hypertensive emergency is defined by severely elevated BP (usually >180/120 mmHg) with evidence of acute end-organ damage: brain (encephalopathy, stroke), heart (ACS, acute HF, aortic dissection), kidneys (acute renal failure), eyes (papilledema, hemorrhages), or vasculature (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia). Headache, visual changes, chest pain, and dyspnea are common presentations.
debrief.pathophysiology: When BP rises above the autoregulatory capacity of vascular beds, endothelial damage occurs, leading to fibrinoid necrosis of arterioles. This triggers a vicious cycle: vascular injury activates RAAS and releases vasoconstrictors, further raising BP. End-organ damage results from both direct pressure injury and ischemia from microvascular thrombosis.
debrief.treatment_overview: Hypertensive emergency requires IV antihypertensives in a monitored setting. Goal: reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour, then to 160/100 mmHg over next 2-6 hours. First-line agents: IV nicardipine, clevidipine, or nitroprusside. Avoid rapid or excessive BP reduction (risk of watershed infarction). Once stabilized, transition to oral medications. Address underlying cause of non-compliance.
high_yield_pearls: Hypertensive EMERGENCY = severely elevated BP + end-organ damage. Hypertensive URGENCY = severely elevated BP WITHOUT end-organ damage.
Do NOT reduce BP too rapidly — goal is 25% reduction in MAP in the first hour to avoid watershed ischemia.
Papilledema and retinal hemorrhages on fundoscopy are key findings that distinguish emergency from urgency.
Schistocytes on peripheral smear indicate microangiopathic hemolysis from severe hypertension.
Medication non-compliance is the most common cause of hypertensive crisis — always address barriers to adherence.
sources: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for High Blood Pressure in Adults
Varon & Marik, Clinical Review: The Management of Hypertensive Crises, Critical Care 2003
Harrisons Principles of Internal Medicine, 21st Edition
